title: Dog Passing Black Tarry Stool (Melena): Causes and Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Black, tarry dog stool (melena) is a serious veterinary emergency indicating upper GI bleeding. Learn what to do and when to call your vet.
content_html:
Understanding Black, Tarry Stool (Melena)

Seeing black, sticky, or tar-like stool in your dog can be very distressing. In the veterinary world, this is known as melena. The dark, ink-like color is caused by the presence of digested blood, which typically means the bleeding is occurring in the upper gastrointestinal tract, such as the stomach or small intestine.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

Black, tarry stool is considered a high-priority medical emergency. Because it indicates internal bleeding, it can lead to rapid blood loss, anemia, and life-threatening shock. If your dog is passing melena, you should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately, especially if they are also showing signs of weakness, pale gums, or vomiting.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

While it may feel unusual, taking a clear photo of the stool before cleaning it up is incredibly helpful for the veterinary triage team. A photo allows the medical staff to assess the color, volume, and consistency of the blood immediately. This visual information helps the vet determine the severity of the internal bleeding and prioritize your dog's care as soon as you arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: Can food or medication cause black stool?
faqs.answer: Yes, certain things like charcoal, iron supplements, or medications containing bismuth can darken stool. However, because internal bleeding is so dangerous, you should always assume it is an emergency until a veterinarian confirms otherwise.

faqs.question: What is the difference between black stool and bright red blood?
faqs.answer: Black stool (melena) indicates blood that has been digested, usually from the stomach. Bright red blood (hematochezia) usually indicates bleeding in the lower colon or rectum.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms of internal bleeding?
faqs.answer: In addition to black stools, watch for lethargy, pale or white gums, a bloated abdomen, loss of appetite, and collapse.
